query: risk of meningiomas in childhood leukemia survivors after cranial radiation therapy

retrieved_docs.title: Low-Grade Gliomas -- Complications -- Radiation-induced Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation-induced tumors can emerge as a result of cranial radiotherapy. It is mainly a concern in the pediatric population, but as cancer therapies improve, a growing cohort of patients may experience radiation-induced tumors. There was a recent large-scale retrospective cohort study of patients that received cranial radiotherapy for pituitary or craniopharyngiomas with over 20 years of follow-up. The patient received a median of 45 to 50.4Gy, similar to what would be prescribed to patients with low-grade gliomas. [64] The 20-year cumulative risk was 4% in radiated patients but 2.1% in non-radiated patients. [64] The vast majority of secondary tumors were benign (88%), with meningioma being the most common. [64]

retrieved_docs.title: Meningioma -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Most meningiomas are sporadic in origin, but some have been associated with certain conditions and risk factors. Environmental factors such as obesity, alcohol use disorder, exposure to ionizing radiation, radiotherapy, hormonal factors such as exposure to exogenous hormones, hormonal replacement therapy, use of oral contraceptive pills, and breast cancer can increase the risk of incidence of meningiomas. [3] [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Carcinomatous Meningitis -- Treatment / Management -- Radiation Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Radiosensitive tumors include hematological malignancies, gynecological tumors, pediatric gliomas, and rhabdomyosarcomas. [38] Focal radiation therapy rarely causes adverse effects. Alopecia, seizures, encephalopathy, mucositis, myelopathy, and myelosuppression can occur with more extensive fields. In patients with good-risk disease (Karnofsky performance status ≥60, no major neurologic deficits, minimal systemic disease), a multimodal approach comprising of both chemotherapy and radiation therapy is preferred. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Germ Cell Seminoma -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications related to treatment have been reported. Exposure to RT results in impairment of neurocognitive functions, vision abnormalities, hypopituitarism, and stroke. An increase in the risk of developing secondary malignancies of CNS like gliomas and meningiomas have also been reported in the patients. CT related side effects are also an important health concern in patients like cytopenia, ototoxicity, and alopecia. [4] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Intraventricular Meningioma -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Meningiomas have progesterone receptors in up to 72% to 90% of the tumors. [2] [3] Female patients may present with worsening symptoms during pregnancy due to elevated blood progesterone levels. [4] The presence of this receptor may explain the significant female predilection seen in sporadic meningiomas. [3] Meningiomas also have estrogen (7%) and androgen (73%) receptors on their membranes. [3] [4] A well-validated factor for increased risk for meningioma development is exposure to ionizing radiation. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Meningioma -- Treatment / Management -- Radiation
retrieved_docs.content: External beam radiotherapy or brachytherapy after surgical resection can be used in grade II and III meningiomas. External beam radiation could improve the overall survival and progression-free survival rates in patients with meningioma. [4] [54] Adjuvant radiotherapy for atypical and malignant meningiomas improves local control with longer progression-free survival and overall survival. [55] [56] Adjuvant radiotherapy after gross total resection of atypical meningiomas may decrease the risk for relapse and improve local control. [55] [57] [58]

retrieved_docs.title: Meningioma -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The tumor grade in meningioma is considered a significant prognostic factor in patients undergoing radiation treatment after surgery. [65] A higher 3-year overall survival was associated with grades 1 and 2. The 5-year overall survival after the use of radiotherapy is approximately 81% associated with grade 1, whereas 53% with grade 2 and 3 meningiomas.

retrieved_docs.title: Meningioma -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Unfortunately, there is no primary prevention of meningiomas has been identified yet. Although meningioma can occur in any individual, some might be at higher risk of developing meningioma. Medical history and neurological examination should be obtained to look for the commonly associated risk factors, including continuous estrogen therapy, exposure to radiation, first-degree relatives with meningioma, and patients with genetic syndromes such as neurofibromatosis type 2. In those high-risk groups of individuals, counseling should be given. Physicians should inform the patients about the chances of developing a meningioma. After the diagnosis of asymptomatic meningioma, regular neurosurgical follow-ups and radiological scans are essential to address the prognosis and outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Astrocytoma -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: No underlying cause has been identified for the majority of primary brain tumors, and the only established risk factor is exposure to ionizing radiation. [1] Children who receive prophylactic radiation for acute lymphocytic leukemia may have a 22 times higher risk of developing a central nervous system (CNS) malignancy within about 5 to 10 years. It has been shown that pituitary adenoma radiation therapy carries 16 times more risk of glioma development. [2] An association with other factors like exposure to electromagnetic fields (cellular telephones), head injury, or occupational risk factors is unproven. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Short Stature -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: As growth hormone has a permissive role in tissue growth, concerns were raised regarding an increased risk of primary malignancy, malignancy recurrence, or secondary malignancy in cancer survivors. A recent consensus concluded, "Current evidence does not support an association between growth hormone replacement and primary tumor or cancer recurrence. The effect of growth hormone therapy on secondary neoplasia risk is small compared to host- and tumor treatment-related factors. Evidence does not support an association between growth hormone replacement and increased mortality from cancer among growth hormone-deficient childhood cancer survivors. Patients with pituitary tumors or craniopharyngioma remnants receiving growth hormone replacement do not need to be treated or monitored differently from those not receiving growth hormone." [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Medulloblastoma -- Radiation Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation therapy is often used for local control and management of the micrometastatic disease. Radiation is administered to the craniospinal axis. To maximize the response, it should be given within four weeks after surgery. Reducing the dose of radiation to minimize the complications is associated with higher relapse rates and poor outcomes. For those who relapse, the prognosis is dismal. At this stage, additional radiation may not help.

retrieved_docs.title: Foster Kennedy Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Tumors of the pituitary gland have also been reported to cause FKS and are the second-highest cause of cerebral tumors. [14] [15] This risk becomes exacerbated in patients who have undergone surgical resection for pituitary adenomas and radiotherapy as they may subsequently develop complications, including tentorial meningiomas. [16] [17] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Meningioma -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology of meningioma. Describe the common physical signs seen in patients with meningioma. Outline the management options for patients with meningioma. Explain the importance of the interprofessional team in the management of patients suffering from meningiomas. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.content: Radioisotope study of the central nervous system is a broad nuclear‑medicine discipline that includes various specialized imaging applications, one of which is the radionuclide identification of eye tumor. The latter is considered a child or subcategory of the former, illustrating that techniques developed for central nervous system radioisotope imaging can be adapted to detect and evaluate ocular tumors, thereby linking CNS imaging practices with ophthalmic oncologic assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 5 -- Community 2928
retrieved_docs.content: The “Allowed qualifier” designation indicates that radiotherapy is recognized as an appropriate therapeutic option for certain medical conditions but not for others. Radiotherapy is an approved treatment modality for ورم الشفة (lip tumor), where it can be used alone or in combination with surgery to achieve local control of malignant disease. In the context of متلازمة نقص المناعة المكتسبة (AIDS), radiotherapy is not a direct therapy for the immunodeficiency itself; however, it may be employed as an allowed intervention for opportunistic malignancies that can arise in patients with AIDS, such as Kaposi sarcoma or non‑Hodgkin lymphoma. Conversely, radiotherapy is not a standard or appropriate intervention for nervous system congenital anomalies; structural developmental defects of the nervous system are generally managed by surgical, rehabilitative, or supportive measures rather than by radiation. Recognizing these distinctions helps clinicians apply radiotherapy where it is evidence‑based—such as for lip tumors and certain cancers in AIDS—while avoiding its use in conditions like congenital nervous system anomalies where it offers no therapeutic benefit.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2799
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is identified as an etiological factor for several distinct medical conditions, including the bladder fistula known as “ناسور مَثانِيٌّ مَهْبِلِيّ,” the acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (“متلازمة نقص المناعة المكتسبة”), death (“التموت”), nervous system congenital anomalies, cerebral palsy (“فالج”), and cleft lip tumor (“ورم الشفة”). In addition, the same qualifier is associated with radiotherapy (“radioterapie”) as a therapeutic or relevant intervention for each of these conditions. Radiotherapy itself is further linked to nervous system congenital anomalies, death, cerebral palsy, and cleft lip tumor, underscoring its applicability or impact across this spectrum of diseases. This integrated view highlights that the allowed qualifier functions both as a recognized cause and as a treatment‑related concept for a range of serious health issues, emphasizing its importance in understanding disease mechanisms and in planning appropriate therapeutic strategies.

Radioisotope studies of the central nervous system (CNS) are a subtype of nuclear medicine diagnostic procedures; they are situated under the broader group of other nuclear medicine imaging and are themselves a child of examinations using radioactive isotopes. Within this CNS subgroup, radionuclide identification of eye tumors is identified as a more specific child procedure, illustrating the extension of CNS radioisotope techniques to ocular oncology. The framework of examinations using radioactive isotopes also includes stereotactic radiosurgical procedures, which are positioned as a child of this overarching radioactive‑isotope examination category, highlighting the integration of precise radiation delivery techniques within the nuclear medicine domain. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2037
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is a term used in radiotherapy to denote specific clinical conditions that are considered permissible or relevant when evaluating a patient for treatment. The allowed qualifiers for radiotherapy include respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest in adults, as well as عدوى بفيروس معوي (infection with an enteric virus). Recognizing these qualifiers helps clinicians assess patient suitability for radiotherapy, anticipate potential complications, and tailor therapeutic plans accordingly.

Overall, the hierarchy connects general radioactive‑isotope examinations to specific imaging and therapeutic techniques: transmission imaging and myocardial imaging derive from the broad radioactive‑isotope examination; nuclear medicine diagnostic procedures branch into radionuclide localization of tumor and radioisotope studies of the central nervous system; radionuclide imaging actions serve as methods for these procedures; and therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine link diagnostic imaging to radiation‑based treatments such as radiation therapy, intracavitary radiotherapy, and stereotactic radiation surgery. This integrated framework clarifies how diagnostic nuclear‑medicine modalities and therapeutic radiation practices are interrelated, supporting comprehensive patient evaluation and treatment planning.
